Black Basta affiliates exploited ZeroLogon CVE-2020-1472 (CWE-330) for privilege escalation
Black Basta affiliates exploited NoPac CVE-2021-42278 (CWE-20) for privilege escalation

ttps.evidence_snippet: Black Basta affiliates have used spearphishing emails to obtain initial access
Black Basta affiliates exploited ConnectWise vulnerability CVE-2024-1709 to obtain initial access
In some instances, affiliates have been observed abusing valid credentials
Black Basta affiliates exploited ZeroLogon, NoPac, and PrintNightmare for local and Windows Active Domain privilege escalation
Affiliates conducted reconnaissance using utilities with innocuous file names such as Intel or Dell

ttps.technique: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
ttps.evidence_snippet: Black Basta affiliates deployed Backstab to disable EDR tooling and used PowerShell to disable antivirus products
Black Basta affiliates have used PowerShell to disable antivirus products
Black Basta affiliates use SoftPerfect network scanner (netscan.exe) to conduct network scanning
Black Basta affiliates use RDP for lateral movement
Black Basta affiliates use Splashtop, ScreenConnect, and Cobalt Strike beacons to assist with remote access
Black Basta affiliates use credential scraping tools like Mimikatz for privilege escalation
Black Basta affiliates use WinSCP to transfer data from a compromised network to actor-controlled accounts
Black Basta affiliates use RClone to exfiltrate data to cloud services such as Mega
A ChaCha20 algorithm with an RSA-4096 public key fully encrypts files; .basta or random extension appended
Black Basta affiliates use vssadmin.exe to delete volume shadow copies

summary: CISA AA24-131A (May 10, 2024) StopRansomware advisory for Black Basta, a RaaS variant first identified April 2022. As of May 2024, affiliates have impacted over 500 organisations globally across North America, Europe, and Australia, hitting at least 12 of 16 critical infrastructure sectors with particular focus on Healthcare and Public Health. Initial access via spearphishing (T1566), exploitation of ConnectWise CVE-2024-1709 (from February 2024), and valid credentials. Privilege escalation via Mimikatz, ZeroLogon (CVE-2020-1472), NoPac (CVE-2021-42278/42287), and PrintNightmare (CVE-2021-34527). Lateral movement via RDP, PsExec, BITSAdmin, Splashtop, ScreenConnect, and Cobalt Strike beacons. Data exfiltrated via RClone/WinSCP before ChaCha20+RSA-4096 encryption; double-extortion model with Basta News TOR leak site. Ransom notes (readme.txt) provide unique code and .onion contact URL; 10–12 day payment window. IOCs include 28 IPs, 57 domains (47 Cobalt Strike C2 + 10 suspected), and 60 file hashes across Tables 7–11.

timeline.event: Black Basta ransomware variant first identified
timeline.evidence_snippet: Black Basta is considered a ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) variant and was first identified in April 2022

timeline.event: Earliest Cobalt Strike C2 domains observed (technologgies.com, jenshol.com, simorten.com, investmentgblog.net, protectionek.com)
timeline.evidence_snippet: Known Black Basta Cobalt Strike domains observed 1/2/2024 (Table 10)

timeline.event: Black Basta affiliates begin exploiting ConnectWise vulnerability CVE-2024-1709
timeline.evidence_snippet: Starting in February 2024, Black Basta affiliates began exploiting ConnectWise vulnerability CVE-2024-1709

timeline.event: New batch of Cobalt Strike C2 domains observed (onedogsclub.com, wipresolutions.com, recentbeelive.com, etc.)
timeline.evidence_snippet: Known Black Basta Cobalt Strike domains observed 3/4/2024 (Table 10)

timeline.event: CISA AA24-131A advisory published; over 500 organisations globally impacted as of this date
